note: Walton scheme instances (haven.claim-scheme.v0) built for Jorn Erik's hosted-only proposal, one per premise. Each object is schema-valid against ClaimSchemeInstance (CellProtocol/Sources/CellBase/PurposeAndInterest/ClaimSchemeInstance.swift): fields schema/instanceID/kind/claimRef/slotBindings/criticalQuestions; CriticalQuestionState fields cqID/question/status/addressedByClaimRef?/note?. kind raw values and cqIDs come from ClaimSchemeCatalog. Statuses are grounded ONLY in what the plain baseline analysis in te2/c2/wa/outputs actually established. The full catalog CQ set per scheme is included (no CQ silently dropped). Each instance's computed evaluation is embedded under _evaluation for review; it is derived by hand from evaluate() and is not part of the ClaimSchemeInstance schema.

slotBindings.sign: Adjacent-task benchmark scores: Norwegian daily-speech CoPilot (best local Qwen3-8B 79.2%, Gemma4 E4B 75.0%, small locals ~50%) and CoPilot JSON-mode (hosted 73-89%, local lower)
slotBindings.event: Local models are too weak for claim-extraction

criticalQuestions.question: Is the sign a reliable indicator of the claimed event?
criticalQuestions.note: ALREADY-IN-BASELINE. The benchmarks measure an ADJACENT task (daily-speech / JSON-mode), not claim-extraction; text_reliability.py already does deterministic anchoring/extraction so the model only refines; the monotonic-mapping warrant is an explicitly INFERRED, unestablished assumption. Baseline captured this as a counterargument + an isInferred assumption support node. The sign->event link is therefore not established -> undercut. S1 is required to move this to answered.

criticalQuestions.question: Could other events account for the sign equally well?
criticalQuestions.note: NET-NEW. Baseline said the task is adjacent but never enumerated alternative CAUSES of the low scores themselves: JSON/format sensitivity, difficulty of the Norwegian daily-speech set, tiny-model floor (the ~50% cases were the smallest models), prompt design, small-sample noise. These are confounders that S1's current definition-of-done does not require isolating. Deduces a sharpening of S1.

slotBindings.action: Drop local models and route the advisory panel to hosted APIs only
slotBindings.consequence: Lower total cost of ownership once local-model maintenance is counted

criticalQuestions.question: How likely are the cited positive consequences to occur?
criticalQuestions.note: ALREADY-IN-BASELINE. Likelihood is volume-dependent: hosted cost scales with panel call volume while local marginal cost is ~0 once installed, and break-even is unknown. Baseline counterargument already states this. S2 gathers the number.

criticalQuestions.question: Is there evidence that the consequences will follow from the action?
criticalQuestions.note: ALREADY-IN-BASELINE. No TCO model exists (leafAuditStatus needs_external_source_audit). This is the identical gap that already deduced S2. Pure restatement.

criticalQuestions.question: Are there negative consequences that outweigh them?
criticalQuestions.note: PARTLY NET-NEW. The counterbalancing negatives (lost offline/deterministic fallback, lost surface diversity, and data classes hosted-only cannot process at ANY price) exist in the baseline only as ROOT-level counters, not folded into the cost premise. This CQ forces a VALUE-ADJUSTED TCO: hosted-only and hybrid do not deliver the same capability set, so a dollars-to-dollars comparison is ill-posed. S2's definition-of-done (per-call price x volume vs maintenance hours x rate) does not require costing the lost capability -> genuine gap in the subtask spec.

slotBindings.entity: An EU-region-hosted API configuration for the panel

criticalQuestions.question: Does the thing actually have the property used to classify it?
criticalQuestions.note: ALREADY-IN-BASELINE, and WEAKER than baseline. EU region addresses transfer/residency only; DPA, subprocessor chain, retention (30-day unless ZDR), legal basis and data class all gate egress, so the config lacks the classifying property. RISK: baseline already rated this premise CONTRADICTED, a stronger verdict than a scheme 'undercut'. Letting scheme evaluation drive scoring would DOWNGRADE contradicted -> merely-undercut. The scheme undercut must not override the direct contradiction.

criticalQuestions.question: Is the classification based on a precise, non-vague definition?
criticalQuestions.note: ALREADY-IN-BASELINE as substance, NET-NEW as diagnosis. Baseline counterargument #3 lists that 'GDPR' is not one thing (legal basis, DPA, subprocessors, retention, transfer, special-category health). The scheme NAMES the mechanism: the premise equivocates on the vague class term 'GDPR-compliant' and commits a category error - compliance is a property of the whole processing operation and its legal basis, not of a hosting region. Sharper diagnosis, same underlying facts, no new subtask.
